section.title: The Parables of the Kingdom
section.sectionRationale: The third discourse is a string of kingdom parables. In the wake of mounting rejection, Jesus teaches in parables that both reveal and conceal, unveiling how the kingdom of heaven actually comes: sown in a word that meets varied hearts, growing secretly and small at first, mixed with evil until the harvest, worth more than everything, and ending in a final separation.
section.thesis: Through the parables Jesus discloses the mysteries of the kingdom: it comes now in a hidden, contested, growing form rather than in immediate glory, it demands a hearing heart that bears fruit, it is worth selling everything to gain, and it will be consummated at the end of the age in the gathering of the righteous and the judgment of the wicked.
section.frameNote: Seven beats: the parable of the sower; the purpose of parables; the sower explained; the wheat and the weeds; the mustard seed and leaven; the weeds explained and the end of the age; and the treasure, pearl, and net, closing with the trained scribe.

verses.text: That same day Jesus went out of the house and sat by the sea.
Such large crowds gathered around Him that He got into a boat and sat down, while all the people stood on the shore.
And He told them many things in {{parabole|parables}}, saying, “A farmer went out to sow his seed.
And as he was sowing, some seed fell along the path, and the birds came and devoured it.
Some fell on rocky ground, where it did not have much soil. It sprang up quickly because the soil was shallow.
But when the sun rose, the seedlings were scorched, and they withered because they had no root.
Other seed fell among thorns, which grew up and choked the seedlings.
Still other seed fell on good soil and produced a crop, a hundredfold, sixtyfold, or thirtyfold.
He who has ears, let him hear.”
Then the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Why do You speak to the people in parables?”
He replied, “The knowledge of the {{mysterion|mysteries}} of the kingdom of heaven has been given to you, but not to them.
Whoever has will be given more, and he will have an abundance. Whoever does not have, even what he has will be taken away from him.
This is why I speak to them in parables: ‘Though seeing, they do not see; though hearing, they do not hear or understand.’
In them the prophecy of Isaiah is fulfilled: ‘You will be ever hearing but never understanding; you will be ever seeing but never perceiving.

For this people’s heart has grown callous; they hardly hear with their ears, and they have closed their eyes. Otherwise they might see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their hearts, and turn, and I would heal them.’
But blessed are your eyes because they see, and your ears because they hear.
For truly I tell you, many prophets and righteous men longed to see what you see but did not see it, and to hear what you hear but did not hear it.
Consider, then, the parable of the sower:
When anyone hears the message of the kingdom but does not understand it, the evil one comes and snatches away what was sown in his heart. This is the seed sown along the path.
The seed sown on rocky ground is the one who hears the word and at once receives it with joy.
But since he has no root, he remains for only a season. When trouble or persecution comes because of the word, he quickly falls away.
The seed sown among the thorns is the one who hears the word, but the worries of this life and the deceitfulness of wealth choke the word, and it becomes unfruitful.
But the seed sown on good soil is the one who hears the word and understands it. He indeed bears fruit and produces a crop, a hundredfold, sixtyfold, or thirtyfold.”

units.label: The wheat and the weeds
units.note: let both grow until the harvest

verses.text: Jesus put before them another parable: “The {{basileia|kingdom}} of heaven is like a man who sowed good seed in his field.
But while everyone was asleep, his enemy came and sowed weeds among the wheat, and slipped away.
When the wheat sprouted and bore grain, then the weeds also appeared.
The owner’s servants came to him and said, ‘Sir, didn’t you sow good seed in your field? Where then did the weeds come from?’
‘An enemy did this,’ he replied. So the servants asked him, ‘Do you want us to go and pull them up?’
‘No,’ he said, ‘if you pull the weeds now, you might uproot the wheat with them.
Let both grow together until the harvest. At that time I will tell the harvesters: First collect the weeds and tie them in bundles to be burned; then gather the wheat into my barn.’”
He put before them another parable: “The kingdom of heaven is like a mustard seed that a man took and planted in his field.
Although it is the smallest of all seeds, yet it grows into the largest of garden plants and becomes a tree, so that the birds of the air come and nest in its branches.”
He told them still another parable: “The kingdom of heaven is like leaven that a woman took and mixed into three measures of flour, until all of it was leavened.”
Jesus spoke all these things to the crowds in parables. He did not tell them anything without using a parable.
So was fulfilled what was spoken through the prophet: “I will open My mouth in parables; I will utter things hidden since the foundation of the world.”
Then Jesus dismissed the crowds and went into the house. His disciples came to Him and said, “Explain to us the parable of the weeds in the field.”
He replied, “The One who sows the good seed is the Son of Man.
The field is the world, and the good seed represents the sons of the kingdom. The weeds are the sons of the evil one,
and the enemy who sows them is the devil. The harvest is the {{synteleia|end of the age}}, and the harvesters are angels.
As the weeds are collected and burned in the fire, so will it be at the end of the age.
The Son of Man will send out His angels, and they will weed out of His kingdom every cause of sin and all who practice lawlessness.
And they will throw them into the fiery furnace, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.
Then the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father. He who has ears, let him hear.

units.label: Treasure, pearl, and net
units.note: worth everything, and a final sorting

verses.text: The kingdom of heaven is like treasure hidden in a field. When a man found it, he hid it again, and in his joy he went and sold all he had and bought that field.
Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a merchant in search of fine pearls.
When he found one very precious pearl, he went away and sold all he had and bought it.
Once again, the kingdom of heaven is like a net that was cast into the sea and caught all kinds of fish.
When it was full, the men pulled it ashore. Then they sat down and sorted the good fish into containers, but threw the bad away.
So will it be at the end of the age: The angels will come and separate the wicked from the righteous
and throw them into the fiery furnace, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.
Have you understood all these things?” “Yes,” they answered.
Then He told them, “For this reason, every scribe who has been discipled in the kingdom of heaven is like a homeowner who brings out of his storeroom new treasures as well as old.”
When Jesus had finished these parables, He withdrew from that place.

parabole.gloss: Parable, a comparison or story laid alongside a truth to illustrate it. Jesus' parables both reveal the kingdom to those who receive them and veil it from the hardened, so that the same story enlightens the humble and hides truth from the proud.

mysterion.gloss: Mystery, a secret now disclosed. The mysteries of the kingdom are the once-hidden ways God's reign actually comes, granted to the disciples but withheld from those who will not receive; not puzzles to solve but revelation to be given.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, reign, royal rule. The parables repeatedly begin the kingdom of heaven is like, unveiling its present, hidden, growing, and mixed character before its glorious consummation at the end of the age.

synteleia.gloss: The end, or consummation, of the age. The harvest of the parables is this final moment when the Son of Man sends His angels to separate the wicked from the righteous, ending the present overlap of good and evil.

basic.context: After rising rejection, Jesus sits in a boat and teaches the crowds in parables. He tells of a sower whose seed lands on four kinds of soil, of wheat and weeds growing together, of a tiny mustard seed and hidden leaven, of treasure and a priceless pearl, and of a net that catches all kinds of fish. To His disciples He explains that these stories unveil the secrets of the kingdom, while veiling them from hardened hearts.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the parables as a set, each opening a facet of how the kingdom comes. It arrives now, but hidden and small; it meets varied responses; it grows secretly; it lets good and evil coexist until the harvest; it is worth everything; and it ends in judgment. Notice that Jesus explains two parables, giving us the key to reading the rest, and that parables both reveal and conceal by design.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus teaches in parables, stories laid alongside the truth. He gives the disciples the mysteries of the kingdom, secrets now disclosed to those who receive. The kingdom of heaven is His constant subject, and its climax is the end of the age, when the Son of Man will send His angels to separate the wicked from the righteous.
basic.typology: The sower and the harvest draw on the prophets' images of God planting and reaping His people. The mustard tree in which birds nest echoes Old Testament pictures of a kingdom sheltering the nations. And the final separation, the furnace and the shining righteous, fulfills Daniel's vision of the wise shining like the stars.
basic.christ: Jesus is the sower who scatters the word of the kingdom, and He is the Son of Man who will send His angels at the harvest to gather and to judge. He is the treasure and the pearl worth selling everything to gain. And He is the one who reveals the mysteries hidden since the foundation of the world.
basic.god: God's kingdom comes on His terms: not in overwhelming display but quietly, patiently, and irresistibly. He is patient with the mixed field, not uprooting evil prematurely, yet He will surely judge at the end. He grants understanding to the humble and gives more to those who receive, while the hardened lose even what they have.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 6:9-10 is quoted about hearing but not understanding. Psalm 78:2 is the prophecy of speaking in parables. Daniel 12:3 lies behind the righteous shining like the sun. And Ezekiel 17 pictures a tree sheltering the birds, like the mustard seed.
basic.oneStory: Rejected by many, Jesus reveals that the kingdom has come in a way no one expected: hidden in a word, small as a seed, mixed with evil, growing secretly toward a certain harvest. It is worth everything to gain, and it will end in a great separation. The story of God's reign is unfolding now, quietly and surely, until the Son of Man gathers His own and the righteous shine like the sun.

context.literary: This third discourse, closing with the formula in verse 53, is a carefully arranged collection of parables. The sower and the weeds are explained privately; the shorter parables cluster around growth, worth, and judgment. Coming right after the hardening opposition of chapter 12, the discourse explains why the kingdom meets rejection yet still advances, and it shifts Jesus' public teaching decisively into parables.
context.historical: Broadcast sowing scattered seed across paths, rocky patches, and thorns as well as good soil. Darnel, a weed, closely resembles wheat until the grain forms, making early uprooting risky. Mustard grew from a tiny seed into a large shrub; leaven worked invisibly through dough. Hidden treasure and pearl trading were familiar; a great pearl could be worth a fortune.
context.canonical: The parables fulfill Psalm 78:2 and interpret Israel's hardening through Isaiah 6:9-10. They develop the Old Testament imagery of planting, harvest, and the sheltering tree, Ezekiel 17:23, and the final separation echoes Daniel 12:2-3. They portray the already and not yet of the kingdom that runs through the New Testament: present in hidden power, awaiting glorious consummation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To unveil the mysteries of how the kingdom of heaven comes, hidden, contested, growing, and supremely valuable, to explain the varied responses to the word, and to warn of the final separation at the end of the age, calling hearers to receive the word and bear fruit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parables describe the nature and course of the kingdom and are not allegories to be decoded in every detail. Yet they carry a clear summons: to be good soil that hears, understands, and bears fruit, and to value the kingdom above all else. The judgment scenes warn, and the treasure and pearl invite wholehearted response.

debates.question: Why does Jesus teach in parables that both reveal and conceal?

views.label: A gracious revelation to the receptive
views.case: To the disciples, parables open the mysteries of the kingdom; those with a humble, hearing heart are given more, so the parables are a gift of understanding to those who receive Jesus.

views.label: A judicial hiding from the hardened
views.case: Quoting Isaiah 6, Jesus shows that parables also veil the truth from those who have closed their eyes, a righteous response to persistent rejection that leaves them ever hearing but never understanding.

debates.bearing: Both are true at once: the same parable enlightens the humble and hides truth from the proud, so that the form of Jesus' teaching itself sifts hearts.

debates.question: Why are the weeds left to grow among the wheat until the harvest?

views.label: God's patience delays judgment
views.case: Pulling the weeds early would harm the wheat; God withholds final judgment to give time and to protect His people, tolerating the present mixture of good and evil in the world.

views.label: Judgment is certain but reserved for the end
views.case: The delay is not indifference; the harvest will surely come, when the Son of Man's angels separate and judge, so the present coexistence is temporary, not permanent.

debates.bearing: Both hold that believers must live faithfully amid a mixed world, neither presuming to enact final judgment now nor doubting that God will justly judge at the end of the age.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus teaches in parabole, stories laid alongside the truth, which reveal and conceal at once. To the disciples He gives the mysterion, the mysteries, the once-hidden secrets of how God's reign comes, now disclosed to those who receive. His theme throughout is the basileia, the kingdom of heaven, unveiled in its present hiddenness, its secret growth, its mixture of good and evil, and its supreme worth. And its horizon is the synteleia tou aionos, the end of the age, when the Son of Man will send His angels to separate the wicked from the righteous, and the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father.

typology.fulfillment: God's planting and reaping of His people, now centered in the Son of Man who sows the word and gathers at the end, Joel 3:13.

typology.type: The mustard tree sheltering the birds
typology.fulfillment: The kingdom growing to shelter the nations, echoing the great tree of Ezekiel 17:23 and Daniel 4.

typology.type: The righteous shining like the sun
typology.fulfillment: Daniel's promise that the wise will shine like the stars, fulfilled in the glory of the redeemed in the Father's kingdom, Daniel 12:3.

study.christ: Jesus stands at the center of every parable. He is the sower who goes out to scatter the word of the kingdom, and He is the Son of Man who owns the field, the world, and who will send His angels at the harvest to gather His own and judge the rest. He is the treasure hidden in the field and the pearl of surpassing price, worth the glad surrender of everything a person has. And He is the revealer of secrets kept since the foundation of the world, opening the mysteries of God's reign to those who have ears to hear. To find Him is to find the kingdom, and to find the kingdom is to find a joy worth every sacrifice.
study.god: The God of the parables brings His kingdom on His own surprising terms. He does not overwhelm the world with immediate glory but sows a word, plants a tiny seed, hides leaven in the dough, and works quietly and irresistibly toward a certain harvest. He is patient, letting good and evil grow together and giving time before the final reckoning, yet His justice is sure: the end of the age will come, and He will separate and judge. He grants understanding to the humble who receive His Son and gives them more, while the hardened lose even what they have. His reign is both a hidden mystery now and a coming glory in which His children will shine.

crossReferences.note: Ever hearing but never understanding, the hardening Jesus cites to explain teaching in parables.
I will open my mouth in parables, the prophecy Matthew says Jesus fulfills.
The final separation and the wise shining like the stars, behind the righteous shining like the sun.
The tender sprig grown into a great tree sheltering the birds, echoed in the mustard seed.

study.oneStory: In the face of rejection, Jesus reveals that God's kingdom has come in a way no one anticipated: not in crushing glory but hidden in a word, small as a seed, secretly leavening the whole, mixed with evil until a certain harvest. It is the treasure and the pearl worth selling everything to gain, and it moves toward the end of the age when the Son of Man will gather His own and the righteous will shine like the sun. The parables invite every hearer to be good soil, to receive the word, and to find in Christ the kingdom worth more than all the world.

quiz.q: What does the parable of the sower teach about responses to the word?

options.text: The same word meets different hearts, and only the good soil hears, understands, and bears fruit.
options.note: Verses 18-23 explain the four soils and the fruitful heart.

options.text: The word will always fail unless the sower works harder.
options.note: The sower sows the same seed; the difference is the soil, the heart's response, not the sower's effort.

options.text: Everyone who hears the word will eventually bear fruit.
options.note: Three of the four soils do not bear lasting fruit; response varies decisively.

options.text: Only those who never face trouble can be saved.
options.note: The good soil bears fruit even amid a world of trouble; trials expose shallow soil rather than defining the saved.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus teach in parables?

options.text: They reveal the kingdom's mysteries to the receptive and veil them from the hardened.
options.note: Verses 11-15 show parables both granting understanding and fulfilling Isaiah's hardening.

options.text: Only to make His teaching easier for everyone to grasp.
options.note: They do illustrate truth, but Jesus says they also conceal it from hardened hearts, which this misses.

options.text: To entertain the crowds without any real meaning.
options.note: The parables carry weighty meaning about the kingdom, not mere entertainment.

options.text: Because He did not want anyone to understand Him.
options.note: He gives the mysteries to the disciples; the concealing is a judgment on the hardened, not a blanket refusal to be understood.

quiz.q: What does the parable of the wheat and the weeds teach?

options.text: Good and evil grow together until the harvest, when the Son of Man will separate and judge.
options.note: Verses 24-30 and 36-43 show the delayed but certain final separation.

options.text: Believers should root out all evildoers from the world now.
options.note: The owner forbids pulling the weeds early; judgment is reserved for the harvest, not enacted by us now.

options.text: The weeds and wheat are actually the same in the end.
options.note: They are decisively separated at the harvest, not treated as the same.

options.text: God is indifferent to the presence of evil.
options.note: The delay is patience, not indifference; judgment is certain at the end of the age.

quiz.q: What do the mustard seed and the leaven reveal about the kingdom?

options.text: It begins small and hidden yet grows and spreads until its effect is vast.
options.note: Verses 31-33 picture the kingdom's secret, pervasive growth from tiny beginnings.

options.text: The kingdom will always remain small and insignificant.
options.note: The opposite; from small beginnings it grows large and permeates the whole, so this misses the growth.

options.text: The kingdom comes only through sudden, visible power.
options.note: These parables stress hidden, gradual growth, not sudden display.

options.text: The kingdom depends entirely on human effort to expand.
options.note: The seed grows and the leaven works by their own hidden power, picturing God's work, not mere human effort.

quiz.q: What do the treasure and the pearl teach about the kingdom's worth?

options.text: It is worth joyfully selling all one has to gain.
options.note: Verses 44-46 show both men gladly giving everything for the surpassing treasure.

options.text: It can be obtained cheaply and without any cost.
options.note: The kingdom is a free gift, yet these parables stress it is worth surrendering everything, which this denies.

options.text: It is valuable only to the wealthy who can afford it.
options.note: The value is the kingdom itself; the point is glad, total commitment, not personal wealth.

options.text: It is one good thing among many equally worthy pursuits.
options.note: The parables present it as supremely valuable, worth more than everything else combined.

questions: The same seed fell on four soils with very different results. What kind of soil is your heart right now, and what would it take to become good soil that bears lasting fruit?
The kingdom often comes hidden and small, like a seed or leaven, rather than in overwhelming display. Where are you tempted to despise God's quiet, gradual work and demand something more dramatic?
Jesus said the wheat and weeds grow together until the harvest. How does trusting God's timing for final judgment free you from bitterness while you live faithfully in a mixed world?
The man sold everything with joy to gain the treasure. What would it look like for you to treasure the kingdom above all your other pursuits and possessions?
There is a real end of the age and a real separation. How does the certainty of final judgment sober you and move you to make sure your own heart is good soil?

translationNotes.note: The source separates the yields with a dash, a hundredfold, sixtyfold, or thirtyfold; the clause is rendered here with commas for a clean, dash-free reading, as also in verse 23.
The quotation is Isaiah 6:9-10, describing a people who hear and see but do not understand, applied to those hardened against Jesus.
I will open My mouth in parables quotes Psalm 78:2; Matthew presents Jesus' parabolic teaching as a fulfillment of Scripture.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 13:1-53. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; poem, line-break, and note markers were skipped. The em dashes in verses 8 and 23 (introducing the yields) were converted to commas. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
